article.title: FCC Votes to Dismantle Copper Retirement Barriers, Clearing the Path for Carriers to Abandon Legacy Networks
article.summary: The FCC's March 26 vote on the Network and Services Modernization Order would eliminate key regulatory hurdles for retiring copper infrastructure, but labor unions and rural advocates warn millions could lose reliable service.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The Federal Communications Commission is scheduled to vote on March 26 on the Network and Services Modernization Order, a regulatory overhaul that would eliminate several longstanding procedural barriers preventing telecommunications carriers from retiring their copper wire networks. FCC Chairman Brendan Carr released the [draft order on March 4](https://www.fcc.gov/document/fcc-accelerating-rollout-modern-high-speed-networks), framing the action as necessary to "free up billions of dollars in private capital so that Americans in communities across the country can go from old and slow copper lines to modern, high-speed ones."

The order arrives as copper subscriptions have [dropped 81 percent between 2014 and 2024](https://docs.fcc.gov/public/attachments/DOC-419252A1.txt), 76 percent of U.S. adults now live in wireless-only households according to CDC data, and carriers face billions in annual maintenance costs for aging infrastructure. But the scale and speed of the proposed deregulation have drawn sharp opposition from labor unions, consumer advocacy groups, and rural residents who argue the FCC is removing safeguards before adequate replacement services are guaranteed.

## What the Order Changes

The Network and Services Modernization Order targets three categories of regulation that have historically slowed the copper retirement process.

**Network change disclosure filings.** The order [eliminates all filing requirements](https://docs.fcc.gov/public/attachments/DOC-419252A1.txt) under Section 251(c)(5) that previously required incumbent local exchange carriers to notify the FCC and obtain approval before making network changes, including copper retirements. Carriers would still be required to post public notices on accessible websites and directly notify interconnected service providers, but the formal FCC filing process and the associated public comment and objection periods would be removed.

**Section 214 discontinuance streamlining.** The order replaces two existing tests for evaluating service discontinuance applications — the Adequate Replacement Test and the Alternative Options Test — with a single consolidated framework. It also extends the 31-day automatic grant period, previously available only to non-dominant carriers, to all discontinuance applications. The order defines [five categories of replacement services](https://docs.fcc.gov/public/attachments/DOC-419252A1.txt) that qualify for streamlined processing, including facilities-based interconnected VoIP, mobile wireless service meeting a 5/1 Mbps minimum threshold, and voice service funded through modernized high-cost support programs.

**Blanket grandfathering authority.** The order grants blanket Section 214(a) authority for carriers to grandfather legacy voice services, lower-speed data telecommunications services, and interconnected VoIP provisioned over copper wire, removing the need for individual authorization.

The order also asserts that federal law preempts state and local requirements that force carriers to continue maintaining legacy networks after obtaining FCC authorization to discontinue those services — a provision that could override state-level consumer protection rules in jurisdictions that have imposed their own copper retirement conditions.

## The Financial Calculus

The economic argument for accelerating copper retirement is substantial. AT&T reported roughly [$6 billion in annual direct operating costs](https://truthonthemarket.com/2026/03/18/subsidizing-obsolescence-how-fcc-rules-keep-copper-alive/) to maintain its copper network in 2024, approximately 5 percent of its $122 billion in annual revenue, while serving a shrinking base of 3.3 million copper lines. Verizon has migrated 4.5 million circuits from copper to fiber and fully retired 36 central offices, generating approximately $180 million in annual operating savings, according to the same [analysis](https://truthonthemarket.com/2026/03/18/subsidizing-obsolescence-how-fcc-rules-keep-copper-alive/).

The FCC's own data underscores the market shift. As of the end of 2024, 79 percent of retail fixed voice connections were interconnected VoIP, up from 58 percent in 2018. The Commission cited 91 residential fixed broadband connections per 100 households at speeds of 25/3 Mbps or greater, and noted that 95 percent of the U.S. population has 4G LTE coverage from three or more providers.

Jeffrey Westling of the International Center for Law and Economics argued in a March 18 [analysis](https://truthonthemarket.com/2026/03/18/subsidizing-obsolescence-how-fcc-rules-keep-copper-alive/) that outdated intercarrier compensation rules create perverse incentives for legacy carriers to maintain copper rather than upgrade, and that phasing out Connect America Fund ICC support could save over $300 million annually — nearly 7 percent of the high-cost program budget. Eighty-six percent of rural local exchange carrier customers are already served by fiber-to-the-premises networks, Westling noted, suggesting the transition is more advanced than critics acknowledge.

The Machine Herald previously reported on the broader telecom investment landscape, with [AT&T pledging $250 billion over five years](/articles/2026-03/20-att-pledges-250-billion-over-five-years-to-rebuild-us-network-infrastructure-for-the-ai-era) to modernize its U.S. network infrastructure. That commitment includes plans to reach 60 million fiber passings by 2030 and to begin decommissioning copper facilities in approximately 500 wire centers in June 2026.

## The Opposition

Criticism of the order has coalesced around three concerns: emergency reliability, rural service gaps, and the pace of transition.

The Communications Workers of America called earlier FCC copper retirement actions "a giant step backward," citing the example of Fire Island, New York, where Verizon replaced damaged copper landlines with wireless service that proved incompatible with health monitors, alarm systems, fax machines, and equipment for hearing-impaired users. The CWA warned that the FCC's approach "puts millions of Americans, particularly in rural areas, at risk for the same disruption of critical communications services."

Rural residents who submitted comments to the FCC offered specific accounts of copper's reliability during emergencies. Alison Denning, a wildfire survivor, told the Commission that her copper landline "continued to be reliable" during recent fires while cell service failed, enabling her to receive alerts and evacuate safely, according to [Broadband Breakfast](https://broadbandbreakfast.com/how-do-we-call-for-help-rural-residents-push-back-on-fccs-copper-retirement/). Janet Tache, a California resident, stated: "I have lived through multiple emergencies when there was no power and only my landline worked." Approximately [69 million Americans still maintain household landlines](https://broadbandbreakfast.com/how-do-we-call-for-help-rural-residents-push-back-on-fccs-copper-retirement/), according to U.S. Chamber of Commerce data from July 2024.

Public Knowledge, the Greenlining Institute, the Utility Reform Network, and the National Association of State Utility Advocates have filed appeals against earlier FCC copper retirement decisions. AARP, representing 38 million Americans aged 50 and older, has also raised concerns about the impact on seniors who depend on traditional phone service.

The CWA has specifically argued that fiber, not fixed wireless, should be the replacement standard, citing greater capacity, predictable performance, lower maintenance costs, and a longer technological lifetime than coaxial cable, satellite, or fixed wireless alternatives. AT&T's copper shutdown strategy has taken what Light Reading described as a "wireless-first approach," using fixed wireless access products rather than fiber as the primary upgrade path for displaced copper customers — a choice the CWA views as a downgrade rather than a modernization.

## What Remains

The order does preserve certain consumer protections. Carriers must notify customers before filing discontinuance applications and at least 30 days before planned service changes. Customers retain the ability to file objections that can remove applications from the streamlined processing track. The Wireline Competition Bureau retains authority to halt automatic grants if concerns arise. Requirements for maintaining 911 connectivity when discontinuing interconnection trunk services remain intact.

However, the elimination of formal FCC filing requirements, public comment periods, and the competitor objection process for network changes removes the primary mechanisms through which consumer advocates, local governments, and competing carriers have historically delayed or modified copper retirement plans. The federal preemption of state and local requirements further narrows the regulatory avenues available to those seeking to impose conditions on the transition.

## Analysis

The Network and Services Modernization Order represents the most significant deregulatory action in U.S. telecommunications since the FCC's 2025 removal of net neutrality rules. Where the net neutrality repeal addressed how carriers treat traffic on their networks, the copper retirement order addresses whether the physical networks themselves must continue to exist.

The underlying market dynamics are clear: copper is a declining technology with declining subscribers and rising maintenance costs, and the vast majority of Americans have already migrated to IP-based voice and broadband services. The question is not whether the copper-to-IP transition will happen, but whether the pace the FCC is enabling will outrun the availability of adequate replacement services in every community — particularly rural areas, low-income neighborhoods, and populations that depend on the electrical independence of copper lines during power outages and natural disasters.

The order's federal preemption clause is likely to draw legal challenges from states that have enacted their own copper retirement protections, adding a federalism dimension to what is otherwise a technical regulatory proceeding. With the vote scheduled for March 26, the Commission appears poised to approve the order along party lines, but the appellate courts that struck down the FCC's net neutrality restoration in January 2025 may ultimately determine how far the Commission's deregulatory authority extends.
